comment.base:
People today don’t seem to understand a basic truth: a country cannot survive without law.
Right now, public discourse in America is driven almost entirely by emotion. Feelings come before facts. Outrage comes before understanding. People rush to conclusions before seeing the full picture, and once a narrative is chosen, evidence no longer matters.
Many Americans have become brainwashed by slogans instead of reality.
People protest without even knowing what they’re protesting for. They repeat talking points, not because they’ve examined the facts, but because everyone around them is saying the same thing. Critical thinking has been replaced by emotional reaction.
I came to the United States because it was a country governed by laws, not feelings.
Yes, I entered illegally many years ago. I don’t hide from that. I took responsibility, followed the process, fixed my status, and today I am a U.S. citizen. That experience is exactly why I value law so deeply.
I have lived in a lawless country.
I know what happens when enforcement disappears and rules stop being taken seriously.
What many Americans don’t understand is this: when you weaken the police, attack enforcement, or call to abolish institutions like ICE, you are not creating compassion — you are creating disorder. And disorder always hurts the poorest, the weakest, and the most vulnerable first.
There is also a serious cultural problem in the U.S. today:
a victim-and-oppressor mindset.
Everyone is taught they are either a victim or an oppressor. Newsflash: most people are neither. Most people are just trying to work, raise families, and live peacefully. But this mindset trains people to see themselves as oppressed even when they live in one of the most privileged, protected societies on Earth.
Here’s what many Americans don’t want to hear:
People in other countries watch what Americans complain about — and they wish they had those “problems.”
They wish they had functional courts.
They wish they had free speech.
They wish they had economic opportunity.
They wish they had safety, infrastructure, and basic stability.
Instead of appreciating those things, Americans protest them.
Today, everything becomes a protest — often without solutions, without context, and without accountability. Disagreement is treated as violence. Facts are treated as hate. And being offended is treated as moral authority.
This is not progress.
This is regression.
If this continues, the United States risks becoming a lawless society driven by emotion, similar to the very places immigrants like me tried to escape.
We need police — not perfect police, but accountable and supported police.
We need borders — because a nation without borders is not a nation.
We need immigration enforcement — because a system without enforcement is dishonest and unsustainable.
Law is not cruelty.
Law is what protects innocent people, workers, legal immigrants, and communities that want peace.
And this needs to be said clearly, because too many people confuse feelings with truth:
Facts do not care about feelings.
And my rights do not end because someone else feels offended.
You can have compassion within a system of law.
But without law, compassion collapses into chaos.
I didn’t come to America for chaos.
And I don’t want to watch it become the very thing so many people around the world wish they had.

I'm English and I'm jealous of the US. 

Yes you're country is a sh1t show right now, yes its scary. 
Yes I will probably feel safer anywhere in the UK, than I would in Portland right now. 

But you still have the right to protest. 

You can be as loud and as obnoxious as you like. 
You can swear at the police, you can speak truth to power, and as much as ICE want to make you afraid of doing that, they know that the courts don't support their attacks, don't support murder, and don't support arresting people for simply speaking the truth. 

THEY DO IN THE UK. 
Our police have arrested THOUSANDS of people since Aprill 2025. Not for hate speech, not for threats, not for vandalism or violence. 

But for saying saying they oppose genocide, and support others who oppose it to. 

I even have to be careful writing this, because if I'm too explicit in this comment I could literally be arrested, as soon as the police read it. 

I feel your pain, at having so many of your rights oppressed by Fascists. But be proud that your justice system doesn't condemn free speech the same way ICE does, and the same way the UK does either.

comment.base:
Forensic Analysis of Video: Shooting of Renee Nicole Good
(Based on visual review of the video provided and DOJ/DHS use-of-force standards)
1. Vehicle Speed and Threat Profile
• The vehicle is moving slowly, not accelerating aggressively.
• There is no visible high-speed movement, ramming behavior, or sudden directional charge consistent with an imminent lethal threat.
• The vehicle’s movement appears controlled and minimal, not weaponized in the legal sense required for deadly force.
Key point:
Slow vehicle movement alone does not meet DOJ or DHS criteria for deadly force.

2. Officer Positioning
• One officer attempts to open the driver’s door.
• Another officer positions himself in front of or alongside the vehicle, rather than disengaging.
• As the vehicle begins to move, the officer steps laterally out of its path.
This is critical:
The officer successfully exits the danger zone before firing.

3. Moment of Discharge
• The shots are fired after the officer has moved out of the vehicle’s direct path.
• At the moment shots are fired:
o The officer is no longer in imminent danger of being struck
o There is clear lateral space available
• The firing appears reactive, not defensive.
Under DOJ policy:
If an officer can move out of the path of a vehicle, deadly force is not justified.

4. Officer-Created Jeopardy
DOJ policy explicitly states:
“Placing oneself in the path of a moving vehicle constitutes officer-created jeopardy and undermines any claim that deadly force was necessary.”
The video shows:
• The officer choosing proximity
• The officer creating risk through positioning
• The officer then using lethal force after disengagement was possible
This directly undermines a self-defense justification.

5. Absence of Alternative Deadly Threat
• There is no visible firearm
• No visible attempt to strike officers with a weapon
• No indication of imminent threat by means other than the vehicle
This fails DOJ’s first allowable condition for firing at a moving vehicle.

6. Policy Alignment Summary
Based on DOJ/DHS standards:
Requirement for Deadly Force Video Evidence
Imminent threat of death or serious injury ❌ Not established
No other reasonable means of defense ❌ Officer moved away
Threat by means other than vehicle ❌ Not present
No officer-created jeopardy ❌ Present

Preliminary Conclusion (Non-Legal)
Based on the video:
• The use of deadly force does not appear consistent with DOJ or DHS policy governing moving vehicles.
• The officer had a clear opportunity to disengage, and did so, before firing.
• This raises serious questions of policy violation, warranting:
o Independent investigation
o Criminal review
o Federal civil-rights inquiry
Whether charges are appropriate is for prosecutors — but policy violations are plainly implicated.

Addendum to Forensic Analysis: Pre-Engagement Behavior
Observed Pre-Contact Conduct
Before any agent dismounted their vehicle:
• Another civilian vehicle passes safely in front of Ms. Good’s vehicle.
• Ms. Good is seen extending her arm out of the driver’s window, gesturing for the passing vehicle to proceed.
• Her gesture is non-threatening, cooperative, and consistent with situational awareness and traffic courtesy.
• There is no erratic movement, aggressive acceleration, or visible attempt to flee at that moment.
This occurs before agents exit their vehicles and initiate direct contact.

Why This Matters
From a use-of-force and threat-assessment perspective, this behavior:
1. Undermines any claim of immediate danger
o Her conduct shows awareness, control, and restraint.
o There is no indication of panic, aggression, or intent to harm.
2. Contradicts a narrative of imminent threat
o A person preparing to use a vehicle as a weapon does not typically pause to allow traffic to pass safely.
o This behavior aligns with non-hostile intent immediately prior to officer engagement.
3. Establishes a calmer baseline before escalation
o Any escalation that follows originates after agents initiate close-contact positioning, not before.
o This is relevant when evaluating who introduced urgency and risk into the encounter.

Policy Relevance
Federal use-of-force standards require officers to continuously reassess threat levels based on observable behavior.
Pre-contact indicators like:
• compliance gestures,
• traffic awareness,
• absence of aggressive movement,
are all mitigating factors that should weigh against the use of deadly force later — not be ignored.

Davenport for Congress 
Legal Team

Subject:
URGENT: Nationwide Communications Blackout in Iran – Risk of Mass Violence
Body:
To whom it may concern,
As of January 9, 2026, the Iranian government has enforced a near-total communications shutdown. Access to the global internet, domestic networks, and mobile, landline, and SMS services has been severely disrupted across the country.
This level of digital isolation has historically been used to conceal state violence. With nationwide protests and a growing public uprising currently underway, the communications blackout raises grave concern that the government intends to suppress dissent through mass violence while preventing documentation, reporting, and international awareness.
By cutting off connectivity, the authorities are not only silencing a population—they are creating conditions to act without witnesses.
We call for immediate and decisive action:
1.  Public condemnation of the nationwide communications blackout.
2.  Coordinated diplomatic pressure demanding the restoration of connectivity without delay.
3.  Mobilization of international mechanisms to deter mass violence and protect civilian lives.
A communications shutdown must be treated as a warning sign, not a future headline. The global community cannot wait for the network to return to learn the scale of harm done in the dark. Action taken now can save lives and uphold fundamental human rights.
We urge you to stand with the people of Iran, whose voices have been forcibly cut off.
Sincerely,
The people of Iran
